coredata.dc:title: In-situ intercalated pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid as an efficient lubricant additive in paraffin oil
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongyu
Xie, Mengxin
Pan, Bingli
Li, Ning
Zhang, Jinghao
Lu, Min
Luo, Jie
Wang, Honggang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel lubricant additive hybridizing pyrolytic graphene and serpentine was reported for the first time to enhance the tribological performance of paraffin oil. The in situ produced pyrolytic graphene from pyrolysis of citric acid was observed to be intercalated into the interlayer of serpentine sheets. The structure, morphology and chemical composition of the composite lubricant additives were characterized by scanning electron microscope, X-ray diffractometer and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. In addition, the anti-wear and friction-reduction mechanisms of the hybrid lubricant additive, owing to its activity on the friction interface, were disclosed by exploring the morphology and component of the wear scar after friction. The tribological results revealed that introducing a small amount of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine lubricant additive (0.2 wt%) to the base oil could effectively reduce the friction coefficient (~87%) and wear loss (~71%) in comparison with neat paraffin oil. First-principles calculations based on density functional theory were further performed to uncover the lubrication mechanism of the hybrid. Therefore, the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid reported herein represents a new strategy to prepare high efficient lubricant additive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrolytic graphene
Serpentine
Lubricant additive
Rubbing surface

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of pyrolytic graphene and pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid
Characterization of the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid
Tribological properties of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid

The authors are grateful to Research Project of 973 ( 2018YFB2000300 ), Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province ( 22A150038 ), Programs for Science and Technology Development of Henan Province ( 222102320065 ) and International Cultivation of Henan Advanced Talents ( 21060004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD patterns and (c) TGA curves of serpentine, pyrolytic graphene and the hybrid.
Mean COF (a) and COF of samples as a function of time (b) in the presence of different additives (0.2 wt%) in paraffin oil: load, 50 N; reciprocating frequency, 10 Hz; test duration, 10 min.
3D profiles of the wear tracks: (a) neat paraffin oil, (b) pyrolytic graphene, (c) serpentine and (d) pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid.
Effect of loads on the mean COF of the graphene/serpentine hybrid.
Worn surface morphology of the disc lubricated with (a) neat paraffin oil, (b) pyrolytic graphene, (c) serpentine and (d) pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid.
EDS patterns of the worn surface lubricated with pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid dispersion under the load of 50 N.
Schematic diagram of preparing pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid and its lubrication mechanism.
Charge difference density map of the most stable adsorption interface between SiO2，Mg2+, graphene and Fe substrate.
DOS diagram for the adsorption of SiO2, Mg2+ and graphene onto the metal substrate.
Literature on the lubricant additives using graphene and serpentine.

head.ce:title: In-situ intercalated pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid as an efficient lubricant additive in paraffin oil

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: H. Liu and M. Xie contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel lubricant additive hybridizing pyrolytic graphene and serpentine was reported for the first time to enhance the tribological performance of paraffin oil. The in situ produced pyrolytic graphene from pyrolysis of citric acid was observed to be intercalated into the interlayer of serpentine sheets. The structure, morphology and chemical composition of the composite lubricant additives were characterized by scanning electron microscope, X-ray diffractometer and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. In addition, the anti-wear and friction-reduction mechanisms of the hybrid lubricant additive, owing to its activity on the friction interface, were disclosed by exploring the morphology and component of the wear scar after friction. The tribological results revealed that introducing a small amount of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine lubricant additive (0.2 wt%) to the base oil could effectively reduce the friction coefficient (~87%) and wear loss (~71%) in comparison with neat paraffin oil. First-principles calculations based on density functional theory were further performed to uncover the lubrication mechanism of the hybrid. Therefore, the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid reported herein represents a new strategy to prepare high efficient lubricant additive.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparing pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid and its lubrication mechanism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction is pervasive in various industrial activities on the contact surface and thus unavoidably results in gigantic energy waste and equipment losses [1,2]. In order to alleviate wear and unnecessary friction so as to prolong the service life of machines, great efforts have been devoted to developing new and efficient lubricants [3–5]. For instance, different metal oxides, sulfides, borides, carbon materials and two-dimensional materials have been widely studied as the additional fillers in lubricants [6–8]. Among them, two-dimensional materials have attracted significant attention due to their high specific surface area, weak intermolecular interactions, ease to enter the contact area between sliding solid surfaces and form tribofilm, and so on. Typically, a large variety of two-dimensional materials such as montmorillonite [9], MoS2 [10], graphite [11], Ti3C2Tx [12], serpentine [13] and graphene [14–16] have aroused strong interest. As described by previous report [17], the coefficient of friction (COF) was determined by the normal load, the real contact area and the shear strength of the tribofilm. In view of this issue, two-dimensional materials are doomed to be extraordinary lubricant additives because they can easily form tribofilm with low shear strength and reduce the interfacial contact area at a certain normal load.

As a burgeoning two-dimensional material, graphene and its derivatives have received considerable interest because of the unique structure, prominent physical and chemical properties [18,19]. Thanks to these elegant advantages, it deservedly has gained increasing attention for improved tribological properties. For example, the tribological properties of graphene [20,21], graphene oxide (GO) [22], modified graphene and GO [23,24] as well as the composites of graphene with other lubricant additives [25,26] have been extensively studied recently. Kinoshita et al. introduced GO monolayer into water-based lubricants [27]. The results demonstrated that GO monolayer greatly improved lubrication performance and resulted in a COF of 0.05. Ren et al. combined graphene with WS2 and introduced the hybrid into perfuoroalkylpolyethers (PFPE). The results indicated that the hybrid exhibited a lower COF than the base oils with individual graphene and WS2 [28]. Despite a tremendous number of works have reported the boosting of nanolubricants by graphene sheets, there are still some challenges restricting their practical applications. For example, most of graphene additives for tribological applications are obtained by the oxidation/reduction method, and on account of the interlayer π-π interaction, the as-prepared graphene sheets tend to form agglomerates during the procedure when graphene is combined with other lubricant materials. Therefore, it is hard for graphene to be efficiently absorbed on the interface between friction pairs [29]. In this regard, it is of great attractiveness to in situ coalesce graphene with other nanomaterials in order to hybridize them at a nanoscale and thus fully maximize their anti-wear and friction reducing performances.

Serpentine, [Mg6 Si4O10 (OH)8], is a kind of natural two-dimensional mineral powder and has found capacious applications in pollutant disposal, energy science and friction-reduction materials, etc [30–33]. Similar to graphene, the weak interlaminar forces between serpentine sheets, scilicet hydrogen bond and van der Waals forces provide serpentine with great convenience for slipping when it undergoes stress. Therefore, serpentine is naturally able to exhibit superior friction-reduction. Furthermore, it has been reported that serpentine has excellent anti-wear properties by forming a self-repairing layer containing iron oxide and silicon oxide on the worn surface [34]. In comparison with other lubricant additives, serpentine has the advantages of low cost, excellent oxidation resistance, high thermal stabilities, good environmental friendliness and outstanding tribological performance [30–33]. Although the employ of serpentine as an efficient lubricant additive has been studied, the in situ resultant graphene/serpentine hybrid as an efficient lubricant additive has never been reported yet.

Herein, we combined serpentine with an in situ produced graphene by pyrolysis of citric acid in order to fully maximize their anti-wear and friction reducing performances for the first time. Compared with graphene derived from the oxidation/reduction method, the bottom-up graphene obtained here via an in situ growth strategy in the gallery of serpentine sheets could efficiently prevent graphene sheets from aggregating because of the protection effect by serpentine sheets. As far as we know, in situ pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid for enhanced tribological performance has never been reported so far. As expected, experimental results exhibited that the hybrid enabled to work as an effective lubricant additive in paraffin oil. The COF declined from 0.13 for neat paraffin oil to 0.017 with only loading of 0.2 wt% hybrid. Moreover, we performed the first principles calculations for additional evidence to elucidate the excellent tribological properties. The outperformance of the hybrid than the individual ones in the fields of friction and wear reduction was attributed to the synergistic effect of serpentine and the in situ generated pyrolytic graphene.

Serpentine powder was obtained from Runchuan mineral powder processing plant, China. Paraffin oil, oleic acid and citric acid were purchased from National Reagent group Co., Ltd., China. Trioctyl trimellitate was purchased from Shandong Yusuo Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. All materials were directly used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of pyrolytic graphene and pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Pyrolytic graphene was prepared as previous report [35]. Briefly, 2 g citric acid was put into a vial and heated to 200 °C for 10 h, resulting in a black powder of pyrolytic graphene. To prepare pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid, citric acid (10.0 g) and serpentine powder (1.0 g) were fully mixed with the assistance of a mortar and pestle. Then, the mixture was sealed into a vessel with holes in the cover to release the pyrolysed gases. Thereafter, it was heated to 200 °C and kept for 10 h. The resulted sample was grinded and rinsed with absolute ethyl alcohol. After sonicated for 30 min, the mixture was centrifuged under 500 rpm for 15 s. The supernatant was then collected and filtered. The obtained filter cake, namely, pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid was dried at 60 °C for 5 h for later use. The hybrid dispersion was thereafter prepared by dispersing it (0.2 wt%) into the paraffin base oil (0.25 wt% oleic acid was employed as a stabilizer) in order to evaluate its tribological performance. For comparison, the pyrolytic graphene dispersion and serpentine dispersion with the same concentration as well as neat paraffin oil were employed as control experiments.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) study was performed using a spectrometer (Nicolet is50, Thermoscientific, USA) for the purpose of proving the combination of the pyrolytic graphene and serpentine. XRD study was carried out using an X-ray diffractometer (D8A, Bruker, USA) with a CuKa target. Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted under a nitrogen atmosphere using Q50 TGA (TA instruments, USA) with a heating rate of 10 ºC min−1. The morphologies of the wear scar and the additive were performed on a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-IT100, Japan). The 3D topography of the wear track after friction test by a ring-on-block friction tester was measured by an optical profilometer (Bruker Contour GT-K, Bruker Nano Inc., USA). The adsorption models and friction repair properties of graphene/serpentine are obtained by using the DMol3 module in Materials Studio software. The tribological properties of the paraffin oil lubricated with graphene/serpentine were evaluated using a ball-on-disk tribometer (MFT-2000, Rtec Instrunlents Inc., USA). The ball with a diameter 6.35 mm and the disc with 30 mm × 30 mm× 10 mm were composed of stainless steel. Before the friction test, the steel ball and the disk were fully cleaned in petroleum ether under ultrasonication for 30 min. For the sake of preparing a uniform dispersion, a small amount of oleic acid (0.25 wt%) was employed to stabilize pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid in the base oil. The test was performed under the conditions of 50 N load, 5 mm sliding distance and 10 Hz oscillating frequency. The disk after testing was ultrasonically cleansed in acetone to clearly observe the wear scar. The COF was thereupon recorded during the test and the mean values within 10 min were employed to calculate the COF. Every test was repeated three times to obtain an average as the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The successful preparation of the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid was characterized by FTIR, XRD, TGA and SEM. As shown in Fig. 1(a), the peaks at 1764 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1 in pyrolytic graphene exhibited the stretching vibrations of CO and CC groups. The epoxy C-O and alkoxy C-O stretching vibrations were observed at 1226 and 1118 cm−1. Moreover, stretching vibration of C-H at 2950 cm−1 and bending vibration of C-H at 1431 cm−1 manifested that the carbonization of citric acid. These results were in line with previous graphene related reports [35,36]. For serpentine, the sharp peak at 3696 cm−1 was attributed to the inner hydroxyl stretching vibration. Furthermore, the peaks at 1086 cm−1 and 956 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of Si-O group and the peak at 607 cm−1 was corresponding to Mg-O stretching vibration [37,38]. As far as the hybrid was concerned, the presences of the aforementioned CO, CC, C-O, C-H, Si-O and Mg-O peaks indicated the successful combination of pyrolytic graphene and serpentine, demonstrating the pyrolytic graphene had been successfully anchored into the gallery in between serpentine sheets. Notably, in comparison with neat serpentine, the Si-O and Mg-O peaks in the hybrid moved toward a higher wavenumber, indicating the strong interaction between the serpentine and in situ produced graphene sheets.

XRD results in Fig. 1(b) clearly exhibited the intercalation of pyrolytic graphene into serpentine further. The XRD pattern of pyrolytic graphene demonstrated a broad peak centered at 2θ= 18.14° (0.49 nm), elucidating the successful formation of pyrolytic graphene [35,36]. The sharp peaks at 2θ= 12.23°, 24.50° and 35.70° of serpentine agreed well with the crystal planes of (002), (004) and (025) of lizardite (JCPDS No. 22–1163) [39]. Apart from the characteristic peaks of serpentine, the hybrid exhibited a characteristic sharp peak at 2θ= 7.84° (1.13 nm). The increased interlayer spacing of serpentine was the direct proof of the intercalation of the pyrolytic graphene into serpentine, suggesting the successful preparation of the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid. Furthermore, a sharp peak at 2θ= 21.8° was observed, indicating the formation of the sp2 hybridized graphene sheets [36]. The intercalation of citric acid and in situ formation of graphene sheets were further investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). According to the TGA curves in Fig. 1(c), serpentine was thermal stable and exhibited two unconspicuous weight loss processes at 350–420 and 600–700 °C, corresponding to the loss of absorbed water and dehydroxylation reaction, respectively [40]. For pyrolytic graphene, prominent weight loss could be observed after 260 °C due to the pyrolysis of residual oxygen-containing groups from the graphene sheets [36]. In comparison with individual serpentine and pyrolytic graphene, the hybrid exhibited an obvious weight loss at 235–270 °C probably due to the pyrolysis of residual oligomer of citric acid, namely graphene quantum dots or graphene chippings in between serpentine sheets [41]. It was obvious that the small gallery between serpentine sheets greatly restricted the quantity of intercalated citric acid molecules, therefore resulting in smaller graphene sheets. However, neat pyrolytic graphene didn't exhibit this weight loss because pure citric acid molecules could grow up freely and yield big graphene sheets under the experimental conditions. Thus, the decreased decomposition temperature of the hybrid at 235–270 °C in contrast with individual serpentine and graphene provided another proof of the intercalation of citric acid into serpentine and the successful in situ generation of graphene sheets.

The morphologies of pyrolytic graphene, serpentine and the as-prepared hybrid were shown in Fig. S1. As was shown, pyrolytic graphene exhibited obvious aggregation due to the unbounded growth of graphene sheets because of abundant citric acid, leaving a layered structure in Fig. S1(a). The morphology of serpentine was showed in Fig. S1(b) with exhibiting flaky particles with varying sizes. Fig. S1(c) exhibited that pyrolytic graphene/ serpentine hybrid had a laminated structure and the range of the particle size was from 1 to 10 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The mean COF and transient curves of COF as a function of time within 10 min of various specimens were shown in Fig. 2. It could be seen that the COF of base oil was about 0.13. It was obvious that neat base oil might not produce efficient protective films at the friction interface. Apparently there existed direct and/or intermittent contact between the ball and disk, thus dissipating more energy in the form of a higher COF. In comparison with the neat base oil, decreased COFs of individual pyrolytic graphene (0.09) and serpentine (0.08) lubricated paraffin oils were observed due to the protection functions of pyrolytic graphene and serpentine. This result was in line with previously reported studies which employed individual graphene or serpentine as lubricating additives [5,20,42]. It was worth noting that the COF was significantly dropped from 0.13 for paraffin oil to 0.017 for pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid dispersion. Obviously, either single graphene or serpentine could not produce such a great reduction in COF (ca. 87%). Synergistic effect should exist among the pyrolytic graphene and serpentine as far as the superior lubrication was concerned. Interestingly, COF curve of the hybrid exhibited a declined tendency with time going on, suggesting the in situ release of embedded superfluous graphene by serpentine. To investigate the effects of lubricating oils after long-time operation, friction coefficient curves for neat base oil and the oil containing the hybrid lubricating additive (0.2 wt%) were obtained and the results were shown in Fig. S2. As was shown, neat base oil exhibited a similar COF value to that in Fig. 2. Slight differences in Figs. 2(b) and S2 were probably due to the systematic error. As for the hybrid, a far smaller mean COF than neat oil could be observed in Fig. S2, demonstrating the efficiency of the hybrid lubricating additive. Meanwhile, minimal fluctuation in COF as time going on could be observed, presumably resulted from the inevitable agglomeration of graphene sheets. It should be pointed out that majorities of graphene and serpentine sheets remained a good dispersion revealed by the smaller COF. The content of lubricant additives was optimized as 0.2 wt% according to the optical images of wear scars on the worn surfaces. As shown in Fig. S3, when the content of the hybrid was 0.2 wt%, the corresponding abrasion loss was the smallest (Table S1). Furthermore, all worn surfaces exhibited severe wear debris except loading of 0.2 wt% hybrid. Thus, the hybrid content was optimized as 0.2 wt%. The tribological results inspired us to further disclose the lubrication mechanism of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid with more characterizations.

Fig. 3 showed the 3D profiles on the wear tracks lubricated with neat base oil, pyrolytic graphene, serpentine and pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid dispersions, respectively. It was disclosed that the surface in Fig. 3(a) lubricated by neat base oil was severely worn with apparent scratch lines and deep furrows presented along the sliding direction in wear tracks (ca. 12 µm in depth). However, the wear tracks lubricated by pyrolytic graphene in Fig. 3(b) and serpentine in Fig. 3(c) exhibited much narrower and shallower furrows, which were observed as 6.8 and 7.6 µm in depth, respectively. The convex peak in Fig. 3(b) was attributed to the wear debris, demonstrating the abrasion occurred to some extent. By contrast, the wear track lubricated by the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid in Fig. 3(d) exhibited the shallowest furrows ca. 2.2 µm in depth due to the formation of efficient tribofilm on the sliding surface. Hence, the 3D profile images of wear tracks further demonstrated the anti-wear ability was greatly improved by addition of pyrolytic graphene/ serpentine hybrid. The outperformance of the anti-wear ability by the hybrid than single graphene and serpentine straightforwardly showed the existence of a synergistic lubrication effect between pyrolytic graphene and serpentine.

When comparing the lubrication performances of the graphene/serpentine hybrid under different loads, it could be found in Fig. 4 that the COF performed worse at lower loads, which agreed with previous work [43]. The increased load resulted in a lower COF, indicating that the specimen showed a negative wear. In the process of friction, the heterogeneous graphene/serpentine hybrid changed from large particles into small particles and the tiny particles could have chemical or physical interaction with the surface of the friction pair. For instance, Si, Mg and other characteristic elements of the hybrid reacted with the wear surface, forming iron and magnesium oxides, silicates, iron carbides, hydrated oxides and graphitized carbon and other material phases, so as to improve its anti-wear and friction-reduction performances [44]. Thus, it was reasonable to conclude that the graphene/serpentine hybrid exhibited the best anti-wear and friction-reduction properties due to the synergistic effect of the in situ produced and intercalated graphene and serpentine sheets.

Taking on account of the larger COF of the base oil, it was reasonable that the friction pairs took more energy to move the worn steel ball against the disk surface, therefore severe abrasion and adhesion occurred during the sliding process, producing deep grooves, spalling and numerous pits on the surface of steel disk shown in Fig. 5(a). However, the worn surfaces lubricated with individual pyrolytic graphene and serpentine dispersions elucidated marginal pitting and grooves shown in Fig. 5(b and c), illustrating the evident alleviation of the wear traces. Especially, the carbonaceous particles (black dots wrapped in white areas) were observed in Fig. 5(b), suggesting the formation of the patchy carbonaceous tribofilm. On the other hand, the worn surface lubricated by the serpentine dispersion exhibited some pitting and shallow furrows due to the big serpentine particles. As far as the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid was concerned, the slightest furrows and the smallest wear scar instead of deep plows, distinct pitting and spalling presented on the worn surface of the steel disk (Fig. 5d), confirming the superior anti-wear properties of pyrolytic graphene/ serpentine hybrid because of the formation of effective tribofilm on the sliding surface.

To better understand the lubrication behavior of pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid, EDS mapping of wear scar was performed and the results were shown in Fig. 6. It was easy to observe that the worn surface in Fig. 6(a) exhibited a smoother morphology with tiny furrows and grooves, illustrating high efficiencies of anti-wear and friction reduction of the as-prepared hybrid. It was noteworthy that the uniform presence of carbon in Fig. 6(b) revealed the formation of carbonaceous tribofilm. Especially, a slight agglomeration behavior of carbon was highlighted with a yellow rectangle at the furrow area attributing to the adsorption of graphene. Namely, the worn surfaces were repaired by filling in the furrows with pyrolytic graphene sheets. Due to the lubrication effect of graphene [45] and the great decrease in COF shown in Fig. 2, the as-formed carbonaceous tribofilm could significantly reduce the friction and wear of the friction pairs [46]. The presence of the O element in Fig. 6(c) was also attributed to the transfer of the hybrid onto the surface of the friction pair. Furthermore, the uniform dispersion of Mg element in Fig. 6(d) suggested that the adsorption of serpentine onto the worn surface and formation of serpentine protective tribofilm [34]. The adsorptions of C and Mg onto the rubbing surface confirmed the formation of the tribofilm, therefore restricting the direct contact of steel ball to disk. On account of the aforementioned results, it was reasonable to conclude that a small amount of the as-prepared hybrid could greatly improve the anti-wear properties due to the adsorption of the C and Mg onto the friction interface with forming the protective tribofilm. We will further confirm this adsorption of the C and Mg onto the friction interface and formation of tribofilm by theoretical calculations later. In addition, the intercalation of pyrolytic graphene into serpentine sheets can prevent graphene sheets from forming agglomeration and sedimentation, therefore yielding an improved tribological performance. The super-robust lubrication performances shown in this study are competitive to the reports listed in Table 1. The hybrid in this study can significantly decrease the friction and wear by 87% and 71%, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 6, the formation of carbonaceous and serpentine tribofilm and their synergistic effect are accountable for the excellent tribological performances.

The in situ production of the pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid and its lubrication mechanism were schematically shown in Fig. 7. Firstly, due to the capillary effect in between serpentine sheets, the citric acid molecules enabled to be absorbed into the interlayer of serpentine. Then, when the temperature increased, the adsorbed citric acid molecules were in situ pyrolyzed to graphene sheets [35], forming intercalated pyrolytic graphene/serpentine hybrid. Thus, under the lubrication condition, the hybrid particles began to be tattered and release the inside graphene sheets. With the aid of lubricating oil, in situ released graphene and serpentine shreds were transported and absorbed onto the rubbing surfaces, forming carbonaceous and serpentine tribofilm. The synergistic effect of the pyrolytic graphene and serpentine toward friction reducing and anti-wear properties resulted from the following two factors: physical boundary film and chemical reaction film. On the one hand, attributed to the lubricating character of graphene and huge specific surface area, the released graphene sheets were able to enter the contact area and were preferentially absorbed onto the boundary surface, forming a physical boundary film on the friction pair, therefore avoiding their direct contact. Furthermore, carbon based physical boundary film could isolate water and oxygen molecules from the friction pair, eliminating the oxidation of the matrix. On the other hand, the crushed serpentine sheets were also adhered to the contact surface, and further tribochemically reacted with the Fe atoms on the worn surface, resulting in a chemical reaction film [51]. Doubtlessly, the newly generated chemical reaction film and carbonaceous physical boundary film exhibited excellent friction reducing and anti-wear properties and repaired the worn surface as well [33]. Herein, the physical boundary film and chemical reaction film exhibited a simultaneous and synergistic effect, thus facilitating the smallest mean COF of hybrid in Fig. 2 and the shallowest furrows in Fig. 3.

In order to further understand the details about how the pyrolytic graphene and serpentine sheets acquired the synergistic effect toward the anti-wear and friction reducing properties, density functional theory (DFT) was used to investigate the adsorption capacity of pyrolytic graphene and serpentine on friction surface based on the first principles. The structure of the hybrid was further addressed by means of the Milligan charge distribution, the density of states (DOS) and other electronic structure changes. The exchange-correlation function was addressed by the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) induced generalized gradient approximation (GGA) method. On the one hand, Mg2+ ions of serpentine were absorbed by citric acid molecules and further decorated onto the in situ produced pyrolytic graphene sheets (as illustrated in Fig. S4) due to the charge neutralization. On the other hand, SiO2 was induced closer to graphene sheets on account of the electrostatic attraction as shown in Fig. S5. The map of charge difference density and corresponding molecular model were shown in Figs. 8 and S6, respectively. The distances between SiO2, Mg2+ and graphene sheets were calculated to be 1.94 Å and 1.72 Å, respectively. The SiO2/Mg2+/graphene protective layer exhibited a distance of 1.96 Å to the metal substrate.

Further, it was demonstrated in Fig. 8 that Fe atoms on the surface of substrate showed charge transfer phenomena. Electrons migrated from Fe atoms to graphene sheets. Therefore, the pyrolytic graphene sheets were firmly absorbed by the metal substrate, forming the aforementioned physical boundary film. The most stable structure of the physical boundary film on metal substrate was schematically illustrated in Fig. S7 and corresponding separation work of the physical boundary film out of metal substrate was calculated to be 2.35 J/m2. Moreover, the formation of the physical boundary film was revealed by the DOS diagram shown in Fig. 9. The appearance of the sharp peak located at the left side of the Fermi level indicated the spontaneity of the formation of the physical boundary film. The difference in peaks of graphene/Mg2+/SiO2/Fe and Fe irrefutably demonstrated the adsorption of graphene/Mg2+/SiO2 onto the metal substrate as a tribofilm. Thus, we have successfully elucidated that the formation of carbonaceous and serpentine based tribofilm on the rubbing surfaces by both experiment and theoretical calculation.

A novel lubricant additive was prepared by in situ combining pyrolytic graphene and serpentine for enhancing the tribological properties of paraffin oil. The as-prepared hybrid lubricant exhibited advanced tribological performances due to the formation of carbonaceous and serpentine based tribofilm. 3D profiles on the wear tracks exhibited furrows of 2.2 µm in depth for the hybrid and 12 µm for neat base oil. The COF declined from 0.13 for paraffin oil to 0.017 with only loading of 0.2 wt% of the hybrid, proving its excellent anti-friction performance. Overall, a synergistic effect of the pyrolytic graphene and serpentine on the friction surface was responsible for the excellent tribological properties. Thus, this work provides a new idea for developing lubricant additive for paraffin oil.

Hongyu Liu: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Mengxin Xie: Investigation, Data curation. Jinghao Zhang: Investigation, Calculation, Visualization. Ning Li: Investigation. Min Lu: Investigation. Jie Luo: Investigation. Bingli Pan: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Honggang Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51675162, 51675509, U1704144, U1504830). The authors are grateful to Research Project of 973 (2018YFB2000300), Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (22A150038), Programs for Science and Technology Development of Henan Province (222102320065) and International Cultivation of Henan Advanced Talents (21060004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129929.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
